q: A Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA) is formally defined as a 5-tuple. Which of the following correctly lists all five components?
options: (Q, Î£, Î´, q0, F) where Q=states, Î£=alphabet, Î´=transition function, q0=start state, F=final states
(Q, Î£, Î´, F, q0) where Q=states, Î£=alphabet, Î´=transition relation, F=final states, q0=start state
(Q, Î“, Î´, q0, F) where Q=states, Î“=stack alphabet, Î´=transition function, q0=start state, F=final states
(Q, Î£, Î´, q0, F) where Q=states, Î£=alphabet, Î´=transition relation (non-deterministic), q0=start state, F=final states
explanation: A DFA is defined as (Q, Î£, Î´, q0, F): Q is a finite set of states, Î£ is a finite input alphabet, Î´: QÃ—Î£â†’Q is the transition function (total, deterministic), q0 âˆˆ Q is the unique start state, and F âŠ† Q is the set of accept/final states. Option B has wrong order. Option C uses Î“ (stack alphabet) which belongs to PDA. Option D incorrectly says Î´ is non-deterministic.

q: In the formal definition of a DFA, the transition function Î´ has the signature:
options: Î´: Q Ã— Î£ â†’ Q
Î´: Q Ã— Î£* â†’ Q
explanation: In a DFA, Î´: Q Ã— Î£ â†’ Q maps a state and a single input symbol to exactly ONE next state. This is what makes it deterministic â€” no choice, no Îµ-transitions. Option B (â†’ 2^Q, the power set) is the NFA transition signature. Option C takes a string Î£* instead of a single symbol. Option D allows Îµ-transitions and multiple states, which is the Îµ-NFA definition.

q: Which of the following is the correct transition function signature for a Non-deterministic Finite Automaton (NFA)?
options: Î´: Q Ã— Î£ â†’ Q
Î´: Q Ã— Î£* â†’ Q
Î´: Q Ã— Î£ â†’ Q âˆª {âˆ…}
explanation: In an NFA, Î´: Q Ã— Î£ â†’ 2^Q maps a state and an input symbol to a SET of states (the power set 2^Q). This allows zero, one, or multiple transitions on the same symbol, making it non-deterministic. Option A is the DFA signature. Option C takes a string. Option D is not a standard definition â€” the empty set âˆ… is already a member of 2^Q, so option B already handles the 'no transition' case.

q: A DFA has states Q = {q0, q1, q2}, alphabet Î£ = {0, 1}, start state q0, and final state F = {q2}. The transition table is: Î´(q0,0)=q1, Î´(q0,1)=q0, Î´(q1,0)=q2, Î´(q1,1)=q0, Î´(q2,0)=q2, Î´(q2,1)=q2. Which string is accepted?
explanation: Trace each string: '01': q0â†’(0)â†’q1â†’(1)â†’q0. Ends in q0, not final. Rejected. '10': q0â†’(1)â†’q0â†’(0)â†’q1. Ends in q1, not final. Rejected. '00': q0â†’(0)â†’q1â†’(0)â†’q2. Ends in q2, which IS a final state. Accepted! '11': q0â†’(1)â†’q0â†’(1)â†’q0. Ends in q0, not final. Rejected. So '00' is accepted.

q: A dead state (trap state) in a DFA is best described as:
options: A state from which no transitions are defined
A non-final state that has transitions only to itself on all input symbols
The start state when it is also a final state
A state that is never reached from the start state
explanation: A dead state (also called trap state or sink state) is a non-final state that loops back to itself on every input symbol. Once the DFA enters this state, it can never reach a final state, so any string processed from this point will be rejected. Option A is wrong â€” a dead state does have transitions (to itself). Option C describes an accepting start state. Option D describes an unreachable state, which is different.

q: In a complete DFA (one where Î´ is a total function), what is the purpose of adding a dead/trap state?
options: To increase the number of accepted strings
To ensure every state has a transition defined for every input symbol
To reduce the number of states in the DFA
explanation: A complete or total DFA requires that Î´(q, a) is defined for every state q and every symbol a âˆˆ Î£. When a natural DFA construction leaves some transitions undefined (the automaton 'gets stuck'), we add a dead/trap state and route all undefined transitions to it. This makes Î´ total without changing the language accepted. Options A, C, D are all incorrect â€” adding a dead state doesn't change accepted strings, doesn't reduce states (it adds one), and is never an accepting state.

q: An NFA accepts a string w if:
options: All possible computation paths on w lead to a final state
At least one possible computation path on w leads to a final state
The first computation path explored leads to a final state
The majority of computation paths on w lead to a final state
explanation: An NFA uses existential acceptance: the string w is accepted if THERE EXISTS at least one sequence of choices (non-deterministic transitions) that leads from the start state to some final state after reading all of w. Even if 999 paths out of 1000 reject, the NFA accepts if one path accepts. A DFA has only one path (deterministic), but NFA explores all paths in parallel conceptually.

q: The Îµ-closure of a state q in an Îµ-NFA is defined as:
options: The set of states reachable from q by reading exactly one Îµ
The set of states reachable from q by zero or more Îµ-transitions
The set of states reachable from q by reading any string
The set of states that can reach q by Îµ-transitions
explanation: Îµ-closure(q) is the set of ALL states reachable from q by taking zero or more Îµ (epsilon) transitions. It always includes q itself (zero Îµ-transitions). For example, if qâ†’Îµâ†’pâ†’Îµâ†’r, then Îµ-closure(q) = {q, p, r}. Option A says 'exactly one Îµ' which misses the transitive closure. Option C is too broad. Option D reverses the direction â€” Îµ-closure follows forward transitions from q, not backward.

q: How is Îµ-closure({q0, q1}) computed when Îµ-closure(q0) = {q0, q2} and Îµ-closure(q1) = {q1, q3}?
explanation: The Îµ-closure of a SET of states is the union of Îµ-closures of each individual state in the set. So Îµ-closure({q0, q1}) = Îµ-closure(q0) âˆª Îµ-closure(q1) = {q0, q2} âˆª {q1, q3} = {q0, q1, q2, q3}. Option A ignores Îµ-reachable states. Option C omits q0 and q1 themselves. Option D incorrectly uses intersection.

q: Which of the following statements about DFA and NFA is TRUE?
options: NFAs can recognize languages that DFAs cannot
DFAs and NFAs recognize exactly the same class of languages (regular languages)
NFAs are strictly more powerful than DFAs
DFAs can have Îµ-transitions just like NFAs
explanation: This is a fundamental theorem in automata theory: DFAs and NFAs are equivalent in expressive power. Every NFA can be converted to an equivalent DFA using the subset construction (powerset construction). Both recognize exactly the class of regular languages. NFAs may be exponentially more concise in terms of state count, but not more powerful. DFAs by definition do NOT have Îµ-transitions.

q: During the subset construction (NFA to DFA conversion), the states of the resulting DFA are:
options: The same states as the NFA
Subsets of the NFA's state set (elements of 2^Q)
Only the reachable states of the NFA
explanation: In the subset construction algorithm, each state of the resulting DFA corresponds to a SUBSET of NFA states. If the NFA has n states, the DFA can have up to 2^n states (hence 'powerset construction'). Each DFA state tracks which set of NFA states the machine could currently be in. In practice, many subsets may be unreachable and can be discarded.

q: A DFA for the language 'all binary strings ending in 1' over Î£={0,1} needs at minimum how many states?
explanation: We need exactly 2 states: q0 (last symbol seen was 0, or no symbol yet â€” not accepting) and q1 (last symbol seen was 1 â€” accepting). Transitions: Î´(q0,0)=q0, Î´(q0,1)=q1, Î´(q1,0)=q0, Î´(q1,1)=q1. Start: q0. Final: {q1}. This correctly accepts strings ending in 1. One state is insufficient (can't distinguish). Three or more would be redundant.

q: In a transition table for a DFA with alphabet Î£ = {a, b}, each row represents a state and each column represents an input symbol. How many entries does a complete transition table have for a DFA with 4 states?
explanation: A complete DFA transition table has exactly |Q| Ã— |Î£| entries â€” one entry per (state, symbol) pair. With 4 states and 2 symbols (a, b), that's 4 Ã— 2 = 8 entries. Each entry contains exactly one state (since DFA is deterministic). If any entry were missing, the DFA would be incomplete (partial).

q: Consider a DFA where the start state is also the only final state, and it has a self-loop on all input symbols. Which language does this DFA accept?
explanation: If the start state is the only final state AND has self-loops on all symbols, the automaton starts in the final state and STAYS in the final state regardless of input. So every string (including Îµ) is accepted. Wait â€” re-read: the start state loops to itself always, so after reading any string we're back in the (final) start state. This accepts ALL strings. But the question says 'only the empty string Îµ' is B â€” that would require leaving the final state and never returning. The correct answer is A: all strings. The DFA never leaves the final state, so it accepts Î£*.

q: Which of the following properties is UNIQUE to DFAs and does NOT apply to NFAs?
options: Can be represented using a transition table
Has a finite set of states
The transition function returns exactly one state for each (state, symbol) pair
explanation: The defining characteristic of DFA (Deterministic) vs NFA (Non-deterministic) is that DFA's Î´: QÃ—Î£â†’Q returns EXACTLY ONE next state for every (state, input symbol) pair. In an NFA, Î´: QÃ—Î£â†’2^Q can return zero, one, or multiple states. All other options (transition table, finite states, start state) apply equally to both DFA and NFA.

q: An NFA has states {q0, q1}, alphabet {a}, start state q0, final state {q1}, and transitions: Î´(q0,a)={q0,q1}, Î´(q1,a)={}. Which strings does this NFA accept?
options: All strings of the form a^n (nâ‰¥1), i.e., a, aa, aaa, ...
explanation: Trace: 'a': q0â†’{q0,q1}. After reading 'a', we're in {q0,q1}. q1 is final â†’ ACCEPT. 'aa': from q0, read first aâ†’{q0,q1}. Read second a: Î´(q0,a)={q0,q1} and Î´(q1,a)={}. Union: {q0,q1}. q1 âˆˆ final â†’ ACCEPT. Similarly any a^n (nâ‰¥1) keeps reaching q1 as a possibility. The key: after each 'a', q1 is reachable, so all non-empty strings of a's are accepted. Îµ is not accepted (q0 not final).

q: What is the key difference between an NFA and an Îµ-NFA?
options: Îµ-NFA has more states than NFA
Îµ-NFA allows transitions on the empty string Îµ, NFA does not
NFA accepts more languages than Îµ-NFA
Îµ-NFA is deterministic while NFA is non-deterministic
explanation: An Îµ-NFA (epsilon-NFA) is an NFA that additionally allows Îµ-transitions: transitions that consume NO input symbol. The automaton can spontaneously change state without reading any input. A standard NFA only allows transitions on actual input symbols from Î£. Both Îµ-NFA and NFA (and DFA) recognize the same class â€” regular languages. Îµ-NFAs are sometimes more convenient for construction (e.g., Thompson's construction for regex).

q: The language accepted by a DFA M=(Q,Î£,Î´,q0,F) is formally defined as:
options: L(M) = {w âˆˆ Î£* | Î´*(q0, w) âˆˆ F}
L(M) = {w âˆˆ Î£* | Î´(q0, w) âˆˆ F}
L(M) = {w âˆˆ Î£+ | Î´*(q0, w) âˆˆ F}
L(M) = {w âˆˆ Î£* | Î´*(q0, w) âˆ‰ F}
explanation: The language of a DFA uses the EXTENDED transition function Î´*: QÃ—Î£*â†’Q (also written Î´Ì‚), which processes an entire string. L(M) = {w âˆˆ Î£* | Î´*(q0, w) âˆˆ F} â€” the set of all strings over Î£* such that starting from q0, after processing w, the DFA is in a final state. Option B uses Î´ instead of Î´* (Î´ only handles single symbols). Option C uses Î£+ which excludes Îµ. Option D negates the condition, giving the complement language.

q: The extended transition function Î´* for a DFA satisfies which recursive definition?
options: Î´*(q, Îµ) = q  and  Î´*(q, wa) = Î´(Î´*(q, w), a)
Î´*(q, Îµ) = {q}  and  Î´*(q, wa) = Î´(Î´*(q, w), a)
Î´*(q, Îµ) = q0  and  Î´*(q, wa) = Î´*(Î´(q, a), w)
Î´*(q, Îµ) = F  and  Î´*(q, wa) = Î´(q, a)
explanation: The extended transition function Î´* is defined recursively: (1) Base case: Î´*(q, Îµ) = q â€” reading the empty string keeps you in the same state. (2) Recursive case: Î´*(q, wa) = Î´(Î´*(q, w), a) â€” to process string wa, first process w from q to get an intermediate state, then apply Î´ on symbol a. Option B returns a set (NFA style). Option C starts from q0 incorrectly. Option D is nonsensical.

q: A DFA is constructed to accept binary strings divisible by 3. The states represent remainders {0,1,2}. What is Î´(q1, 0)?
explanation: In the 'divisible by 3' DFA, state qi represents the remainder i when the current number is divided by 3. Appending bit b to a number n gives new value 2n+b. From q1 (remainder=1): reading 0 gives 2Ã—1+0=2, remainder=2. So Î´(q1,0)=q2. Reading 1 gives 2Ã—1+1=3, remainder=0. So Î´(q1,1)=q0. This is a classic DFA construction problem in IBPS/GATE exams.

q: In a DFA transition table, a state marked with 'â†’' typically indicates:
options: A state with no outgoing transitions
explanation: In standard automata textbook notation for transition tables, 'â†’' (arrow) marks the START state (initial state), and '*' or double circle marks the final/accepting state(s). Some books use 'â†’*' for a state that is both the start and a final state. Knowing these notations is important for correctly reading transition tables in exam questions.

q: How many states does the minimal DFA for the language L = {Îµ} (only the empty string) over Î£ = {a, b} have?
explanation: The minimal DFA for L = {Îµ} needs 2 states: q0 (start AND final state, accepts Îµ) and q_dead (trap state, non-final). From q0, reading any symbol (a or b) goes to q_dead. From q_dead, reading any symbol stays in q_dead. This ensures only Îµ is accepted. One state is insufficient â€” if the single state is final, it accepts all strings; if non-final, it accepts nothing.

q: Which of the following is NOT a valid property of a DFA?
options: It always halts on finite inputs
It can be in multiple states simultaneously
It processes input left to right, one symbol at a time
Its transition function is total (defined for all state-symbol pairs in a complete DFA)
explanation: Being in multiple states simultaneously is a property of NFAs (conceptually), not DFAs. A DFA is ALWAYS in exactly ONE state at any point during computation. This is the core meaning of 'deterministic' â€” no ambiguity about the current state. All other options are valid DFA properties: it always halts (finite input processed in finite steps), processes left to right, and a complete DFA has a total transition function.

q: An NFA with n states, when converted to a DFA using subset construction, can have at most how many states?
explanation: The subset construction creates DFA states that correspond to subsets of the NFA's state set Q. Since Q has n states, it has 2^n subsets (the power set 2^Q), giving a DFA with at most 2^n states. In the worst case, all 2^n subsets are reachable and needed. However, often many subsets are unreachable and the actual DFA is much smaller. This exponential blowup is a key exam fact.

q: Consider the NFA with states {q0,q1,q2}, alphabet {a,b}, start q0, final {q2}, and Î´(q0,a)={q0,q1}, Î´(q0,b)={q0}, Î´(q1,b)={q2}, Î´(q1,a)={}, Î´(q2,a)={}, Î´(q2,b)={}. What language does this NFA accept?
explanation: The NFA accepts strings where at some point it guesses (non-deterministically) to move from q0 to q1 on 'a', then from q1 to q2 on 'b'. Before the guess, it can stay in q0 on any symbols. So the pattern is: any prefix (any a's and b's keeping it in q0), then 'a' (move to q1), then 'b' (move to q2, final). This is exactly (a|b)*ab â€” all strings ending in 'ab'. Options A and D are the same here, and the answer is D which spells it out formally.

q: The start state of a DFA/NFA is:
options: May or may not be a final state
Always the state with the most transitions
explanation: The start state (initial state) q0 can independently be a final state or not â€” there's no constraint linking these two roles. If q0 âˆˆ F, then the empty string Îµ is accepted by the automaton (since Î´*(q0, Îµ) = q0 âˆˆ F). If q0 âˆ‰ F, then Îµ is rejected. Both configurations are valid. For example, the DFA for 'all strings including Îµ' has q0 as final; the DFA for 'non-empty strings' has q0 as non-final.

q: In a DFA, what happens when the input string is fully consumed and the current state is NOT a final state?
options: The DFA loops back to the start state
The DFA continues processing with Îµ
The DFA non-deterministically picks another path
explanation: In a DFA, acceptance is determined solely by the state after ALL input is consumed. If the DFA finishes reading the entire string and the current state is not in F (the set of final states), the string is REJECTED. There is no backtracking, looping to start, or Îµ-continuation in a DFA. This is a fundamental principle â€” the DFA makes one linear pass and gives a yes/no answer based on the final state reached.

q: Which of the following correctly describes the relationship between the language classes recognized by DFA, NFA, and Îµ-NFA?
options: L(DFA) âŠ‚ L(NFA) and L(Îµ-NFA) is incomparable
explanation: All three models â€” DFA, NFA, and Îµ-NFA â€” are equivalent in expressive power. They all recognize exactly the class of REGULAR LANGUAGES. This is proven by: (1) every DFA is trivially an NFA, (2) every NFA is trivially an Îµ-NFA (with no Îµ-transitions), (3) every Îµ-NFA can be converted to NFA (by computing Îµ-closures), and (4) every NFA can be converted to DFA (subset construction). So all three classes are equal.

q: A transition table row for NFA state q with input 'a' shows '{q1, q2}'. This means:
options: The NFA must go to both q1 and q2 simultaneously, creating two parallel computations
The NFA is in an error state
The NFA deterministically picks q1 over q2
This is invalid â€” NFA rows can only contain single states
explanation: In an NFA, the transition function returns a SET of states. When Î´(q, a) = {q1, q2}, the NFA non-deterministically branches into two parallel computation paths: one continuing from q1 and another from q2. Conceptually, the NFA 'clones' itself and pursues all possibilities. If ANY branch eventually reaches a final state after consuming all input, the string is accepted. This is the power of NFA non-determinism.

q: For Îµ-NFA conversion to NFA, which step correctly handles the new transition function?
options: Î´_NFA(q, a) = âˆª_{p âˆˆ Îµ-closure(q)} Îµ-closure(Î´_ÎµNFA(p, a))
explanation: To convert Îµ-NFA to NFA, the new transition is: Î´_NFA(q, a) = Îµ-closure of all states reachable by: first taking all Îµ-transitions from q (Îµ-closure(q)), then reading symbol a from each of those states, then taking all Îµ-transitions from the result. Formally: Î´_NFA(q, a) = âˆª_{p âˆˆ Îµ-closure(q)} Îµ-closure(Î´_ÎµNFA(p, a)). This captures the effect of Îµ-moves before and after reading each symbol.

q: A DFA has 3 states: q0 (start), q1, q2 (final). The alphabet is {0,1}. Transition: Î´(q0,0)=q0, Î´(q0,1)=q1, Î´(q1,0)=q2, Î´(q1,1)=q1, Î´(q2,0)=q2, Î´(q2,1)=q2. Is the string '101' accepted?
options: The DFA crashes on this input
explanation: Trace '101' step by step: Start at q0. Read '1': Î´(q0,1)=q1 â†’ now at q1. Read '0': Î´(q1,0)=q2 â†’ now at q2. Read '1': Î´(q2,1)=q2 â†’ now at q2. End of string. Current state is q2, which IS a final state. Therefore, '101' is ACCEPTED.

q: In the context of DFA construction, a 'dead state' is added when:
options: The number of states exceeds the alphabet size
A transition from a state on some symbol is not defined in the original (incomplete) automaton
A state has no incoming transitions
The start state has no outgoing transitions
explanation: A dead state is added to make a DFA COMPLETE (total transition function). When constructing a DFA, sometimes a state naturally has no transition defined for certain input symbols (the automaton would 'get stuck'). To formalize this, we add a single dead/trap state and redirect all undefined transitions to it. The dead state has self-loops on all symbols. This is necessary for the formal definition of DFA which requires Î´ to be defined for all (state, symbol) pairs.

q: Which string is in the language of the DFA with: states={q0,q1,q2,qdead}, start=q0, final={q2}, Î´(q0,a)=q1, Î´(q0,b)=qdead, Î´(q1,a)=qdead, Î´(q1,b)=q2, Î´(q2,a)=qdead, Î´(q2,b)=qdead, Î´(qdead,a)=qdead, Î´(qdead,b)=qdead?
explanation: This DFA accepts only 'ab'. Trace 'ab': q0â†’(a)â†’q1â†’(b)â†’q2 âœ“ (final). Trace 'ba': q0â†’(b)â†’qdead. Dead. Trace 'aab': q0â†’(a)â†’q1â†’(a)â†’qdead. Dead. Trace 'abb': q0â†’(a)â†’q1â†’(b)â†’q2â†’(b)â†’qdead. Dead. So only 'ab' is accepted. The language is L = {ab}.

q: The number of final states in a DFA:
options: Can be 0 or more (including all states being final)
Must equal the number of non-final states
explanation: There is NO restriction on the number of final states in a DFA. F âŠ† Q, so F can be the empty set âˆ… (DFA accepts no strings â€” the empty language), any proper subset of Q, or all of Q (DFA accepts all strings â€” Î£*). A DFA with F=âˆ… recognizes the empty language âˆ…. All options A, B, D are wrong â€” the definition only requires F âŠ† Q.

q: Consider NFA M with states {q0,q1}, alphabet {a,b}, Î´(q0,a)={q0,q1}, Î´(q0,b)={q0}, Î´(q1,a)=âˆ…, Î´(q1,b)=âˆ…, start=q0, final={q1}. The equivalent DFA (via subset construction) for the subset {q0,q1} will be a _____ state.
explanation: In subset construction, a DFA state (subset S) is a FINAL state if S contains at least one NFA final state. The subset {q0,q1} contains q1, which is a final NFA state. Therefore, the DFA state corresponding to {q0,q1} is a final state. When the NFA could be in q0 or q1 simultaneously (after reading 'a' from q0), and since q1 is final, the DFA accepts at this point.

q: Which of the following statements about Îµ-closure is FALSE?
options: Îµ-closure is computed using a BFS or DFS from the given state following only Îµ-transitions
Îµ-closure(q) may equal {q} if there are no Îµ-transitions from q
Îµ-closure(q) can be empty if q has no Îµ-transitions
explanation: Îµ-closure(q) is NEVER empty because it always includes q itself (by convention, you can reach q from q by taking ZERO Îµ-transitions). So option D is FALSE. Option A is TRUE (q âˆˆ Îµ-closure(q) always). Option B is TRUE (BFS/DFS following Îµ-transitions). Option C is TRUE (if q has no outgoing Îµ-transitions, Îµ-closure(q) = {q}, which is not empty).

q: A DFA M accepts string w. What can we conclude?
options: M rejects the complement of w
w must have length greater than 0
M accepts all strings longer than w
explanation: By definition, a DFA accepts string w if and only if Î´*(q0, w) âˆˆ F â€” the extended transition function starting from the initial state q0, after processing w, lands in a final (accepting) state. Option A is confused â€” the complement of a string isn't meaningful in this context (complement of a LANGUAGE is). Option C is wrong â€” Îµ is a valid accepted string if q0 âˆˆ F. Option D is completely wrong â€” no such implication exists.

q: In a transition table, the entry Î´(q2, b) = q2 means:
options: State q2 is a final state
State q2 has a self-loop on input 'b'
State q2 is the start state
State q2 rejects all strings containing 'b'
explanation: Î´(q2, b) = q2 means when the DFA is in state q2 and reads input symbol 'b', it transitions back to q2. This is a SELF-LOOP â€” the state transitions to itself. Self-loops are common in dead/trap states (self-loops on all inputs) and also in states that need to 'stay put' while waiting for a specific future input. This does NOT tell us whether q2 is final or the start state.

q: The language L = {w âˆˆ {a,b}* | w contains at least one 'a'} â€” what is the minimum number of states needed in a DFA?
explanation: Minimum 2 states: q0 (start, no 'a' seen yet â€” non-final) and q1 ('a' has been seen â€” final). Transitions: Î´(q0,a)=q1, Î´(q0,b)=q0, Î´(q1,a)=q1, Î´(q1,b)=q1. Once we've seen an 'a', we stay in q1 regardless of further input. This 2-state DFA correctly accepts all strings containing at least one 'a'. One state is insufficient because we can't distinguish 'seen a' from 'not seen a'.

q: For an NFA, which of the following is a valid transition in the transition function?
options: Î´(q0, a) = q1 only (exactly one state)
Î´(q0, a) = âˆ… (empty set, no transition)
Both B and C are valid NFA transitions
explanation: In an NFA, Î´: Q Ã— Î£ â†’ 2^Q (power set). This means the transition function can return ANY subset of Q, including: the empty set âˆ… (no valid transition â€” the computation path dies), a singleton {q1} (behaves like DFA on this transition), or a set with multiple states {q1, q2, q3} (non-deterministic branching). All are valid. Option D (both B and C) is correct.

q: What does it mean for two finite automata M1 and M2 to be equivalent?
options: They have the same number of states
They have the same transition structure
They accept exactly the same language: L(M1) = L(M2)
They have the same set of final states
explanation: Two automata (DFA, NFA, or Îµ-NFA) are equivalent if and only if they accept exactly the SAME LANGUAGE. L(M1) = L(M2) means every string accepted by M1 is accepted by M2 and vice versa. They can have completely different numbers of states, different transition structures, and different final state sets â€” as long as they recognize the same set of strings. This is the foundation of NFA-to-DFA conversion: produce an equivalent DFA.

q: A DFA for 'binary strings with even number of 1s' has state q0 (even count, start and final) and q1 (odd count). What is Î´(q1, 0)?
explanation: In the 'even number of 1s' DFA: q0 = even count of 1s (including zero), q1 = odd count of 1s. Reading '0' does NOT change the count of 1s. So: Î´(q0, 0) = q0 (even stays even) and Î´(q1, 0) = q1 (odd stays odd). Reading '1' flips the parity: Î´(q0, 1) = q1 and Î´(q1, 1) = q0. So Î´(q1, 0) = q1.

q: In the formal 5-tuple definition of an NFA (Q, Î£, Î´, q0, F), which component differs from the DFA definition?
explanation: The ONLY difference in the 5-tuple definition between DFA and NFA is the transition function Î´. In DFA: Î´: Q Ã— Î£ â†’ Q (returns exactly one state). In NFA: Î´: Q Ã— Î£ â†’ 2^Q (returns a set of states). All other components â€” Q (finite set of states), Î£ (finite alphabet), q0 âˆˆ Q (start state), F âŠ† Q (final states) â€” are defined identically in both.

q: Which of the following languages CANNOT be recognized by a DFA?
options: All strings of balanced parentheses like ((()))
All strings over {a,b} with even length
explanation: Balanced parentheses requires counting the nesting depth, which can be arbitrarily large. A DFA has FINITE memory (finite states), so it cannot count to arbitrary depth. The language of balanced parentheses is a CONTEXT-FREE language, not regular â€” it requires a pushdown automaton (PDA). The other three options are regular: 'ending in 01' needs 3 states, 'even length' needs 2 states, 'contains ab' needs 3 states.

q: When constructing a DFA to recognize strings over {0,1} that end with '01', what is the minimum number of states required?
explanation: Minimum 3 states: q0 (start: last chars not matching any prefix of '01'), q1 (last char was '0' â€” matched first char of '01'), q2 (last two chars were '01' â€” accepting state). Transitions: Î´(q0,0)=q1, Î´(q0,1)=q0, Î´(q1,0)=q1, Î´(q1,1)=q2, Î´(q2,0)=q1, Î´(q2,1)=q0. Start: q0. Final: {q2}. This correctly accepts strings ending in '01'.

q: An Îµ-NFA has state q0 with Îµ-transitions to q1 and q2. State q1 has an Îµ-transition to q3. What is Îµ-closure(q0)?
explanation: Îµ-closure(q0) includes all states reachable from q0 by zero or more Îµ-transitions: q0 itself (zero transitions), q1 (one Îµ-transition: q0â†’q1), q2 (one Îµ-transition: q0â†’q2), q3 (two Îµ-transitions: q0â†’q1â†’q3). So Îµ-closure(q0) = {q0, q1, q2, q3}. The closure is transitive â€” we follow Îµ-transitions from newly discovered states too (q3 is reachable via q1).

q: A student claims: 'If an NFA has k states, the equivalent DFA always needs exactly 2^k states.' This claim is:
options: Sometimes true, sometimes false â€” 2^k is the MAXIMUM, not always the exact count
Always false â€” DFA always needs fewer states
True only when k is odd
explanation: The claim is SOMETIMES TRUE (worst case) but NOT always. The subset construction can produce up to 2^k states, but many of these subsets may be UNREACHABLE from the start state. Unreachable states are discarded, often resulting in a DFA with far fewer than 2^k states. The exponential blowup (2^k states actually needed) does occur for certain constructed NFA families, but it's the worst case, not the typical case. So 2^k is the upper bound.

q: In a DFA, which of the following is true about the transition function Î´?
options: Î´ is a partial function (may be undefined for some inputs)
Î´ is a total function when the DFA is complete
Î´ can map a state to multiple states
Î´ can take Îµ as input
explanation: For a COMPLETE DFA, Î´: Q Ã— Î£ â†’ Q is a TOTAL function â€” defined for every combination of state and input symbol. This is why we add dead states (to cover undefined transitions). An INCOMPLETE DFA may have Î´ as a partial function, but formally a DFA is usually assumed complete. Options C and D describe NFA and Îµ-NFA respectively. In competitive exams, 'DFA' implies total Î´ unless stated otherwise.

q: The language accepted by the DFA with a single state q0 (which is both start and final), with self-loops Î´(q0,a)=q0 and Î´(q0,b)=q0 over Î£={a,b} is:
explanation: With a single state q0 that is both the start AND final state, and self-loops on all symbols: starting in q0 (final), any input returns to q0 (final). So Î´*(q0, w) = q0 âˆˆ F for ALL strings w âˆˆ Î£*. This includes Îµ (empty string, since we start in q0 which is final). The DFA accepts EVERY string over {a,b}*, which is Î£*.

q: TRAP: An NFA with n states always requires a DFA with MORE states than the NFA. True or False?
options: True â€” subset construction always adds states
False â€” the equivalent DFA may have fewer, equal, or more states than the NFA
True â€” because DFA must be deterministic
False â€” DFA and NFA always have the same number of states
explanation: This is FALSE. The equivalent DFA can have FEWER states than the NFA in some cases. For example, an NFA with unreachable states or redundant non-determinism might convert to a smaller DFA. The DFA can also have MORE states (up to 2^n). Or it can have the same count. There's no fixed relationship â€” it depends on the specific NFA. The statement 'always requires MORE states' is definitively false.

q: In the NFA-to-DFA subset construction algorithm, each state of the resulting DFA corresponds to:
options: A single state of the NFA
A subset of states of the NFA
The power set minus the empty set of NFA states
explanation: In subset construction, each DFA state represents a subset of NFA states. The DFA tracks all possible states the NFA could be in simultaneously after reading the same input. Hence each DFA state is a subset of the NFA's state set.

q: An NFA has n states. What is the maximum number of states the equivalent DFA (obtained by subset construction) can have?
explanation: Each DFA state corresponds to a subset of NFA states. With n NFA states, there are 2^n possible subsets (the power set), so the DFA can have at most 2^n states. This is the state blow-up phenomenon.

q: Which of the following is TRUE about the subset construction of an NFA to DFA?
options: The resulting DFA always has exactly 2^n states where n is the number of NFA states
The resulting DFA is always minimal
Many subsets may be unreachable and the actual DFA may have far fewer than 2^n states
The subset construction always produces a DFA with more states than the NFA
explanation: While theoretically 2^n subsets exist, in practice many subsets are unreachable from the start state. The actual DFA built by subset construction only includes reachable subsets, which is often much less than 2^n.

q: The epsilon-closure of a state q in an epsilon-NFA is:
options: The set of states reachable from q on exactly one epsilon transition
The set of states reachable from q on any number of epsilon transitions including q itself
The set of states reachable from q on any input symbol
The set of states from which q is reachable on epsilon transitions
explanation: The epsilon-closure (Îµ-closure) of a state q is the set of all states reachable from q by following zero or more epsilon (Îµ) transitions. It always includes q itself (zero epsilon transitions).

q: When converting an epsilon-NFA to a DFA, the start state of the DFA is:
options: The start state of the epsilon-NFA
The epsilon-closure of the start state of the epsilon-NFA
The set of all states of the epsilon-NFA
The epsilon-closure of all final states
explanation: The DFA's start state is the epsilon-closure of the epsilon-NFA's start state. This accounts for all states reachable via epsilon transitions before reading any input symbol.

q: In epsilon-NFA to DFA conversion, a DFA state (subset S) is a final/accepting state if:
options: All states in S are final states of the epsilon-NFA
S contains at least one final state of the epsilon-NFA
S is equal to the set of all final states of the epsilon-NFA
S contains the start state of the epsilon-NFA
explanation: A DFA state (subset S) is accepting if and only if at least one state in S is a final/accepting state of the epsilon-NFA. This ensures the DFA accepts the same language.

q: The 'state blow-up' problem in NFA-to-DFA conversion refers to:
options: The NFA having too many epsilon transitions
The DFA potentially having exponentially more states than the NFA
The DFA being nondeterministic after conversion
The loss of some accepting states during conversion
explanation: State blow-up refers to the fact that converting an NFA with n states to a DFA may require up to 2^n states. This exponential increase in the number of states is called state blow-up, and there exist NFAs where this worst case is achieved.

q: There exists an NFA with n states for which the minimum equivalent DFA requires exactly 2^n states. This demonstrates that:
options: Subset construction is incorrect in some cases
The exponential blow-up in subset construction is tight (worst case is achievable)
DFAs are more powerful than NFAs
Every NFA conversion results in exactly 2^n DFA states
explanation: The exponential blow-up is not just a theoretical upper bound â€” it is tight. There exist specific NFAs (e.g., the language of strings where the n-th symbol from the end is '1') for which the minimum DFA requires exactly 2^n states.

q: In DFA minimization using the table-filling algorithm, two states p and q are initially marked as distinguishable if:
options: p and q have different numbers of outgoing transitions
Exactly one of p and q is a final state
Both p and q are final states
p and q transition to the same state on all inputs
explanation: In the table-filling (Myhill-Nerode / marking) algorithm, the base case marks a pair (p, q) as distinguishable if exactly one of them is a final state â€” because any string (including the empty string Îµ) distinguishes them.

q: In the table-filling DFA minimization algorithm, after the base step, we iteratively mark pairs (p, q) as distinguishable if:
options: p and q are both final states
For some input symbol a, the pair (Î´(p,a), Î´(q,a)) is already marked as distinguishable
p and q have different numbers of transitions
p and q are reachable from different start states
explanation: In the iterative step of the table-filling algorithm, pair (p, q) is marked distinguishable if there exists some input symbol 'a' such that Î´(p,a) and Î´(q,a) form a pair already marked as distinguishable. This propagates distinguishability backwards.

q: After running the table-filling DFA minimization algorithm, two states p and q are equivalent (indistinguishable) if:
options: Their pair is marked in the table
Their pair is NOT marked in the table
They both reach the same final state on all inputs
They have transitions to the same set of states
explanation: At the end of the table-filling algorithm, a pair (p, q) that remains unmarked means no string distinguishes them â€” they are equivalent (indistinguishable). Equivalent states are merged to minimize the DFA.

q: DFA minimization merges equivalent states. The resulting minimized DFA is:
options: An NFA that may be smaller than the original DFA
The unique smallest DFA (up to isomorphism) that accepts the same language
A DFA that may accept a slightly different language for efficiency
explanation: DFA minimization produces the unique minimum-state DFA (up to renaming of states) that accepts exactly the same language as the original DFA. This follows from the Myhill-Nerode theorem.

q: Before applying DFA minimization, which step should be performed first?
options: Convert the DFA to an NFA
Remove all unreachable states from the DFA
Add a dead/trap state to the DFA
Convert all final states to non-final states
explanation: Before minimization, unreachable states (states not reachable from the start state on any input) should be removed. These states don't affect the language accepted and their inclusion can distort the minimization process.

q: A Moore machine is a finite state machine where:
options: Output depends on the current state and the current input symbol
Output depends only on the current state
Output depends only on the current input symbol
Output is produced only when the machine reaches a final state
explanation: In a Moore machine, the output is associated with states (not transitions). The output at any point depends solely on the current state, not on the input that caused the transition.

q: A Mealy machine is a finite state machine where:
options: Output depends only on the current state
Output depends on both the current state and the current input symbol
Output is emitted only on epsilon transitions
Output depends only on the input string length
explanation: In a Mealy machine, outputs are associated with transitions. The output depends on both the current state and the current input symbol being processed.

q: Compared to a Moore machine, a Mealy machine for the same task typically has:
options: Exactly the same number of states
explanation: Mealy machines generally require fewer or equal states compared to equivalent Moore machines because outputs are on transitions (more flexible), while Moore machines associate output with states, sometimes requiring state splitting.

q: In a Moore machine, the output string produced for an input string of length n has length:
explanation: In a Moore machine, output is produced at each state visit. For an input of length n, the machine visits n+1 states (including the start state before reading any input), producing an output of length n+1.

q: In a Mealy machine, the output string produced for an input string of length n has length:
explanation: In a Mealy machine, output is produced with each transition. For an input of length n, there are exactly n transitions, producing an output string of length n.

q: Which of the following best describes the formal definition of a Mealy machine?
options: (Q, Î£, Î´, q0, F) where F is a set of final states
(Q, Î£, Î”, Î´, Î», q0) where Î»: QÃ—Î£ â†’ Î” is the output function
(Q, Î£, Î”, Î´, Î», q0) where Î»: Q â†’ Î” is the output function
(Q, Î£, Î´, q0, F, Î») where Î»: Q â†’ Î”
explanation: A Mealy machine is defined as (Q, Î£, Î”, Î´, Î», q0) where Q=states, Î£=input alphabet, Î”=output alphabet, Î´:QÃ—Î£â†’Q is the transition function, Î»:QÃ—Î£â†’Î” is the output function (depends on state AND input), and q0 is the start state. No final states are needed.

q: Which of the following best describes the formal definition of a Moore machine?
options: (Q, Î£, Î”, Î´, Î», q0) where Î»: QÃ—Î£ â†’ Î”
(Q, Î£, Î”, Î´, Î», q0) where Î»: Q â†’ Î”
(Q, Î£, Î´, q0, F) where F âŠ† Q
(Q, Î£, Î”, Î´, q0, F) where F is the output set
explanation: A Moore machine is defined as (Q, Î£, Î”, Î´, Î», q0) where Q=states, Î£=input alphabet, Î”=output alphabet, Î´:QÃ—Î£â†’Q is the transition function, Î»:Qâ†’Î” is the output function (depends only on state), and q0 is the start state.

q: Every Mealy machine can be converted to an equivalent Moore machine. During this conversion:
options: The number of states always decreases
The number of states may increase (state splitting may be needed)
The number of states always stays the same
The conversion is not always possible
explanation: When converting Mealy to Moore, a state q that produces different outputs on different incoming transitions must be 'split' into multiple states (one for each distinct output). This can increase the number of states.

q: The epsilon-closure computation is required in which conversion?
explanation: Epsilon-closure is a concept specific to epsilon-NFAs (NFAs with epsilon transitions). It is computed during epsilon-NFA to DFA conversion to determine which states are reachable via epsilon transitions and must be included in each DFA state subset.

q: An NFA has states {q0, q1, q2} where q0 is the start state and q2 is the final state. The subset construction starts from which subset?
explanation: In subset construction (without epsilon transitions), the DFA's start state is the subset containing just the NFA's start state, which is {q0}. (For epsilon-NFA, it would be the epsilon-closure of q0.)

q: In subset construction, the dead state (empty set âˆ…) is a DFA state that:
options: Corresponds to the NFA's reject state
Is a non-accepting trap state â€” once entered, no accepting state is reachable
Corresponds to all NFA states simultaneously
Is always the start state of the DFA
explanation: The empty set âˆ… (dead/trap state) in subset construction represents the situation where the NFA has no active states. It is a non-accepting state, and all transitions from it lead back to itself. Any string continued from this state will be rejected.

q: Which of the following statements about NFA and DFA is FALSE?
options: Every DFA is a special case of NFA
Every NFA has an equivalent DFA
DFAs can accept languages that NFAs cannot
NFAs and DFAs accept exactly the class of regular languages
explanation: DFAs and NFAs are computationally equivalent â€” they accept exactly the same class of languages (regular languages). DFAs cannot accept any language that an NFA cannot, and vice versa. So the statement 'DFAs can accept languages NFAs cannot' is FALSE.

q: In the table-filling algorithm for DFA minimization, if we have states {A, B, C, D} and pairs (A,C) and (B,D) are unmarked at the end, then the minimized DFA merges:
options: A with B, and C with D
A with C, and B with D
A with D, and B with C
explanation: Unmarked pairs in the table-filling algorithm represent equivalent (indistinguishable) states. If (A,C) and (B,D) are unmarked, then Aâ‰¡C and Bâ‰¡D. The minimized DFA merges A with C into one state, and B with D into one state.

q: A DFA has 5 states: {q0, q1, q2, q3, q4}. After minimization, 2 pairs are found to be equivalent. The minimized DFA has how many states?
explanation: If 2 pairs of states are equivalent, that means 4 states collapse into 2 states (each pair merges into 1). So from 5 original states, we get 5 - 2 = 3 states in the minimized DFA.

q: Which of the following is a key property of the minimized DFA?
options: It is unique up to isomorphism (renaming of states)
It always has exactly half the states of the original DFA
It accepts a superset of the original language
explanation: By the Myhill-Nerode theorem, for any regular language there is a unique minimum-state DFA (up to isomorphism â€” i.e., up to renaming of states). This means all minimization procedures yield the same structure.

q: Two DFAs M1 and M2 accept the same language if and only if:
options: They have the same number of states
They have the same transition structure
They have the same set of final states
explanation: Two DFAs accept the same language if and only if their minimized (canonical) forms are isomorphic. This is a direct consequence of the uniqueness of the minimum DFA for a regular language.

q: What is the epsilon-closure of a state that has no outgoing epsilon transitions?
options: The set containing only that state itself
explanation: Epsilon-closure always includes the state itself (reachable via zero epsilon transitions). If a state has no outgoing epsilon transitions, its epsilon-closure is just the singleton set containing that state.

q: In converting an epsilon-NFA to a DFA, the transition function of the DFA for a subset S on input a is computed as:
options: The union of Î´(q, a) for all q in S
The epsilon-closure of the union of Î´(q, a) for all q in S
The intersection of Î´(q, a) for all q in S
explanation: For DFA state S and input a: first compute all NFA states reachable from any state in S on input a (union of Î´(q,a)), then take the epsilon-closure of that result to account for epsilon transitions after reading 'a'. This gives the next DFA state.

q: An epsilon-NFA has states {0,1,2} with epsilon transitions 0â†’1 and 1â†’2. The epsilon-closure of state 0 is:
explanation: Starting from state 0: we can reach 0 itself (trivially), then 1 via one epsilon transition (0â†’1), then 2 via another epsilon transition (1â†’2). So Îµ-closure(0) = {0, 1, 2}.

q: Which machine model is typically preferred for hardware implementation due to its simpler timing behavior?
options: Neither â€” Turing machines are preferred
explanation: Moore machines are preferred in hardware design because their output depends only on the current state (not on the input), which avoids combinational feedback loops and makes timing analysis simpler and more predictable.

q: A Moore machine for sequence detection generally requires _____ states compared to the equivalent Mealy machine.
explanation: Moore machines generally require more states than equivalent Mealy machines because outputs are tied to states. When a Mealy machine would produce different outputs on transitions entering the same state, the Moore equivalent must split that state.

q: The subset construction algorithm is also known as:
explanation: The subset construction algorithm is also called the powerset construction because the DFA states correspond to elements of the powerset (set of all subsets) of the NFA's state set.

q: Consider an NFA accepting the language 'strings over {0,1} where the second symbol from the right is 1'. The minimum DFA for this language has how many states?
explanation: The language 'second symbol from right is 1' over {0,1} requires the DFA to remember the last two symbols. The minimum DFA has 4 states corresponding to the last two input symbols seen: {Îµ/start, saw_0, saw_1, saw_10_or_11}. Actually the standard result is 4 states for this type of language.

q: In DFA minimization, a 'dead state' (trap state) that only has self-loops:
options: Should always be kept in the minimized DFA
Should be removed if it is non-accepting and never needs to be distinguished
Should be merged with the start state
Should be converted to a final state
explanation: A dead (trap) state is non-accepting and all transitions from it loop back to itself. If not required by the minimization algorithm (i.e., it's not needed to distinguish other states), it can be omitted from the minimized DFA â€” creating a partial DFA. However, for a complete DFA, it must be present.

q: Which of the following conversions is NOT directly possible without intermediate steps?
options: Regular expression â†’ epsilon-NFA â†’ DFA
explanation: Regular expression to DFA is typically done in two steps: first convert the regex to an epsilon-NFA (Thompson's construction), then convert the epsilon-NFA to a DFA (subset construction with epsilon-closure). There is no single-step direct algorithm commonly taught.

q: In the table-filling algorithm, the table has entries for:
options: All unordered pairs {p, q} where p â‰  q
Only pairs where one state is final
explanation: The table in the table-filling (marking) algorithm has entries for all unordered pairs {p, q} where p â‰  q. A state is always equivalent to itself, so same-state pairs are not considered.

q: Which of the following is a distinguishing string for two states p (non-final) and q (final) in a DFA?
options: A string that takes both p and q to non-final states
A string that takes both p and q to final states
explanation: The empty string Îµ immediately distinguishes p and q: reading Îµ from p gives p (non-final = reject), but reading Îµ from q gives q (final = accept). Since one accepts and the other rejects on Îµ, they are distinguished by Îµ.

q: An NFA has n states. The worst case for the number of states after subset construction is 2^n. For n=3, this is:
explanation: For n=3 NFA states, the maximum number of DFA states after subset construction is 2^3 = 8 (corresponding to all subsets of a 3-element set: âˆ…, {q0}, {q1}, {q2}, {q0,q1}, {q0,q2}, {q1,q2}, {q0,q1,q2}).

q: Both Moore and Mealy machines differ from DFAs/NFAs in that they:
options: Produce output strings rather than just accepting or rejecting
explanation: Moore and Mealy machines are transducers â€” they compute output functions, producing an output symbol for each state/transition. Unlike DFAs (which only accept or reject), transducers map input strings to output strings.

q: A Mealy machine and a Moore machine are called equivalent if:
options: They have the same number of states
They produce the same output for every input string
They have the same transition structure
They have the same output alphabet
explanation: Two transducers (Mealy or Moore) are equivalent if for every input string they produce the same output string. The equivalence is defined in terms of input-output behavior, not structural similarity.

q: In subset construction, if the NFA has an epsilon transition, before computing the transition of a subset S on input 'a', we must:
options: Compute Îµ-closure(S) and use that as the effective current set
Ignore states reachable only via epsilon from S
Compute the power set of S
explanation: When computing DFA transitions from subset S on input 'a' in an epsilon-NFA: we take Îµ-closure of the result of moving from all states in S on 'a'. More precisely: Î´_DFA(S, a) = Îµ-closure(âˆª{Î´_NFA(q,a) | q âˆˆ S}). The epsilon-closure must be taken after the 'a' transition.

q: If a DFA has no pair of equivalent states (all pairs are distinguishable), then:
options: The DFA has only one state
The DFA must be converted to an NFA first
The DFA accepts the empty language
explanation: If all pairs of states are distinguishable (no two states are equivalent), then no states can be merged. The DFA is already minimal â€” it cannot be reduced further.

q: The Myhill-Nerode theorem relates to DFA minimization by stating that:
options: Every NFA can be converted to a DFA
The number of equivalence classes of the Nerode right-congruence equals the number of states in the minimum DFA
Every DFA can be minimized to a single state
The minimum DFA has as many states as the number of symbols in the alphabet
explanation: The Myhill-Nerode theorem states that a language is regular if and only if the number of equivalence classes of the right-invariant relation on strings is finite, and this number equals the number of states in the minimum DFA. This is the theoretical foundation of DFA minimization.

q: In NFA-to-DFA conversion, if the NFA has k reachable states in a particular subset, the corresponding DFA state:
options: Represents all k NFA states being active simultaneously
Has k copies of the same transition
Is k steps away from the start state
explanation: A DFA state in subset construction represents a snapshot of all NFA states that could be simultaneously active after reading the same input prefix. If the subset has k states, it means the NFA could be in any of those k states.

q: Which of the following is NOT a component of a Moore machine?
options: An output function Î»: QÃ—Î£ â†’ Î”
A transition function Î´: QÃ—Î£ â†’ Q
explanation: In a Moore machine, the output function is Î»: Q â†’ Î” (output depends only on state). The function Î»: QÃ—Î£ â†’ Î” (output depends on state AND input) is the output function of a MEALY machine, not a Moore machine.

q: After DFA minimization, if the original DFA had a dead (trap) state, the minimized DFA:
options: May merge the dead state with other non-accepting states if they are equivalent
Always keeps the dead state unchanged
Converts the dead state to a final state
explanation: During minimization, the dead/trap state is treated like any other non-accepting state. If it is equivalent (indistinguishable) to other non-accepting states, it will be merged with them. The minimization algorithm applies uniformly.

q: An epsilon-NFA can be converted to a DFA without first converting it to a standard NFA (without epsilons). This is done by:
options: Incorporating epsilon-closures into the subset construction directly
First converting to a regular expression, then to DFA
explanation: We can convert epsilon-NFA directly to DFA by using an extended subset construction that incorporates epsilon-closures. The DFA start state is Îµ-closure(q0), and transitions are computed using Îµ-closure after each move. No intermediate NFA-without-epsilon step is required.

q: Which of the following is NOT a primitive regular expression over alphabet Î£?
options: Any single symbol a âˆˆ Î£
explanation: The primitive regular expressions are: âˆ…, Îµ, and any single symbol a âˆˆ Î£. Î£* is derived â€” it is formed by applying Kleene star to a union of all symbols, not a primitive.

q: What does the operator '|' represent in a regular expression?
explanation: The '|' operator denotes union/alternation. a|b matches either 'a' or 'b'. Concatenation is juxtaposition (ab), Kleene star is *, and complement is not a standard RE operator.

q: What is the language denoted by âˆ…*?
explanation: âˆ…* = {Îµ} because Kleene star always includes zero repetitions (which gives Îµ), and any positive number of repetitions of âˆ… is still âˆ…. So âˆ…* = {Îµ}. This is a classic trap question.

q: Which RE describes all strings over {a,b} that start with 'a'?
explanation: a(a|b)* means exactly one 'a' at the start followed by any string over {a,b}. Option B ends with 'a', Option C has zero-or-more a's then one character, Option D ends with 'a'.

q: The expression (a|Îµ) is equivalent to which notation?
explanation: (a|Îµ) means 'a or empty string' = zero or one 'a', which is the definition of a? (optional a). a* is zero or more, a+ is one or more.

q: How many distinct strings of length 2 does (a|b)(a|b) generate?
explanation: Each position can be 'a' or 'b', giving 2Ã—2 = 4 combinations: aa, ab, ba, bb.

q: Which RE denotes all strings over {0,1} containing an even number of 0s?
explanation: 1*(01*01*)* works: leading 1s handled by 1*, then each group (01*01*) contributes exactly two 0s separated by any 1s, repeated zero or more times. This ensures total 0-count is always even.

q: What does a+b* denote?
options: One or more a's followed by zero or more b's
Zero or more a's followed by one or more b's
explanation: a+ = one or more a's; b* = zero or more b's. Juxtaposition is concatenation, so a+b* = one or more a's followed by zero or more b's. No alternation is implied.

q: L(rÂ·s) is formally defined as:
options: {xy | x âˆˆ L(r), y âˆˆ L(s)}
explanation: Concatenation of languages: L(rÂ·s) = {xy | x âˆˆ L(r) and y âˆˆ L(s)}. Every string in L(rÂ·s) is formed by taking a string from L(r) and appending a string from L(s).

q: (a*b*)* is equivalent to:
explanation: (a*b*)* generates all possible interleavings of a's and b's repeated arbitrarily, which equals (a|b)* â€” the set of all strings over {a,b}. Any string can be split into blocks of a's followed by b's.

q: In regular expression operator precedence, which operator has the HIGHEST precedence?
explanation: Parentheses override all precedence rules and have the highest effective precedence. Among the three standard operators, the order (high to low) is: Kleene star (*) > Concatenation > Union (|). So ab* means a(b*), not (ab)*.

q: In the expression ab*|c, which grouping is correct according to standard precedence?
explanation: Precedence: * binds tightest â†’ b*; then concatenation â†’ ab*; then union â†’ ab*|c = (ab*)|c. So the expression is parsed as (a(b*))|c, not (ab)*|c or a(b*|c).

q: Which algebraic identity holds for regular expressions?
explanation: R|âˆ… = R because union with the empty set adds no new strings. Similarly RÂ·Îµ = R (Îµ is identity for concatenation), and RÂ·âˆ… = âˆ… (concatenation with empty set gives empty set). Option A and D are wrong.

q: Which identity correctly states the role of Îµ in concatenation?
explanation: Îµ is the identity element for concatenation: RÂ·Îµ = ÎµÂ·R = R. Concatenating any language with the language {Îµ} returns the original language unchanged, just as multiplying by 1 in arithmetic.

q: Which of the following is a valid algebraic identity for Kleene star?
explanation: (R*)* = R* because R* already contains all possible repetitions; applying * again doesn't add anything new. R* â‰  R+ (R+ excludes Îµ if Îµ âˆ‰ L(R)); âˆ…* = {Îµ} â‰  âˆ….

q: Which identity relates R+ and R*?
explanation: R+ means one or more repetitions = RÂ·R* = RR*. This is the standard identity: R+ = RR* = R*R. Note R+ = R* only if Îµ âˆˆ L(R).

q: The identity R|R = ?
explanation: R|R = R by the idempotency law of union. Taking the union of a set with itself yields the same set. This mirrors set theory: A âˆª A = A.

q: Which of the following regular expression identities is FALSE?
explanation: R|(SÂ·T) â‰  (R|S)Â·(R|T) in general. Concatenation does NOT distribute over union from outside in this way. The correct distributivity is: concatenation distributes over union: R(S|T)=RS|RT and (R|S)T=RT|ST. Option C is the incorrect 'union distributes over concatenation' which is false for REs.

q: Îµ* equals:
explanation: Îµ* = Îµ because (Îµ)^n = Îµ for all n â‰¥ 0 (concatenating empty string any number of times is still empty string). So the Kleene closure of {Îµ} is just {Îµ}.

q: Which of the following pairs of REs are equivalent?
explanation: (a|b)* = (a*b*)* as shown by the fact that both denote all strings over {a,b}. a*b â‰  ab* (first ends with exactly one b, second starts with exactly one a). (ab)* â‰  a*b* (first has equal a's and b's alternating). a+ â‰  a* (a+ excludes Îµ).

q: When converting the RE (a|b)* to an NFA using Thompson's construction, what is the minimum number of states required?
explanation: Thompson's construction for (a|b)* proceeds: 'a' needs 2 states, 'b' needs 2 states, (a|b) union adds 2 new states = 6 states, and the outer * adds 2 more states = 8 states total. Each atomic RE contributes 2 states; each union and star operator adds 2 states.

q: In Thompson's NFA construction for RE rÂ·s, how are the NFAs for r and s combined?
options: The accepting state of NFA(r) is merged with start state of NFA(s) via Îµ-transition
A new start state is added with Îµ-transitions to both NFA(r) and NFA(s)
The accepting state of NFA(r) is connected to start of NFA(s) by Îµ, and NFA(r)'s accepting state becomes non-accepting
explanation: In Thompson's construction for concatenation rÂ·s: an Îµ-transition is added from the accepting state of NFA(r) to the start state of NFA(s), and the accepting state of NFA(r) is made non-accepting. Options A and C together describe this correctly, making D the answer.

q: The NFA produced by Thompson's construction for any RE has which special property?
options: It has exactly one accepting state and no transitions out of it
It may have multiple start states
It always has at least one cycle
explanation: A key property of Thompson's NFA construction is that the resulting NFA has exactly one start state and exactly one accepting state, with no transitions leaving the accepting state. This simplifies the recursive construction.

q: Converting NFA to DFA is done using which algorithm?
explanation: The subset construction (powerset construction) converts an NFA to an equivalent DFA. Each DFA state corresponds to a subset of NFA states. Thompson's construction converts RE to NFA. Arden's theorem converts FA to RE.

q: In subset construction, if an NFA has n states, the resulting DFA can have at most how many states?
explanation: The subset construction creates DFA states corresponding to subsets of NFA states. With n NFA states, there are 2â¿ possible subsets (including the empty set). In the worst case all 2â¿ subsets are reachable, giving 2â¿ DFA states. In practice, often far fewer are reachable.

q: Which of the following correctly describes RE â†’ DFA conversion via Thompson's method?
explanation: The standard pipeline is: (1) Convert RE to NFA using Thompson's construction, then (2) Convert NFA to DFA using subset construction. There is no direct standard algorithm from RE to DFA in one step, and PDAs/CFGs are for context-free languages.

q: The RE a(a|b)* corresponds to a DFA that accepts:
options: All strings over {a,b} of length â‰¥ 1
explanation: a(a|b)* = one 'a' followed by any string over {a,b} (including Îµ). This accepts exactly all strings that start with 'a'. The start state transitions on 'a' to an accepting state that loops on both a and b.

q: When constructing an NFA for R* using Thompson's construction, how many Îµ-transitions are added compared to the NFA for R?
explanation: For R*, Thompson's construction: adds a new start state q0 and new accepting state qf; adds Îµ from q0 to qf (for Îµ âˆˆ L(R*)), Îµ from q0 to NFA(R)'s start, Îµ from NFA(R)'s accept to qf, and Îµ from NFA(R)'s accept back to NFA(R)'s start (for repetition). That's 4 new Îµ-transitions.

q: Which of the following REs generates the same language as the DFA with states {q0,q1}, start=q0, accept={q0}, Î´(q0,a)=q1, Î´(q1,a)=q0, Î´(q0,b)=q0, Î´(q1,b)=q1?
explanation: The DFA accepts strings with an even number of a's (q0=even a's seen, q1=odd). The RE for even number of a's over {a,b} is: b*(ab*ab*)* = (b*ab*a)*b*, which matches option B. Starting in q0 (even), any b's are fine, pairs of a's (with b's between) keep it even.

q: The language of the RE (ab)* is:
options: All strings of a's and b's alternating
All strings starting with a and ending with b
explanation: (ab)* generates Îµ, ab, abab, ababab, ... â€” exactly strings of the form (ab)^n for n â‰¥ 0. While these strings do alternate a and b, 'all alternating strings' would include ba, baba, etc. â€” so option A is broader than what (ab)* generates. The precise answer is B.

q: Arden's theorem is used to:
options: Prove a language is not regular
explanation: Arden's theorem provides a method to extract a regular expression from a finite automaton by setting up and solving a system of linear equations over regular expressions. It is the algebraic method for FA â†’ RE conversion.

q: Arden's theorem states that if R and S are regular expressions and Îµ âˆ‰ L(R), then the equation X = RX | S has the unique solution:
explanation: Arden's theorem: If Îµ âˆ‰ L(R), then X = RX | S has the unique solution X = R*S. The condition Îµ âˆ‰ L(R) ensures uniqueness. This is analogous to solving x = ax + b â†’ x = a*b in regular expression algebra.

q: Why does Arden's theorem require Îµ âˆ‰ L(R) for uniqueness?
options: Because R* is undefined when Îµ âˆˆ L(R)
Because when Îµ âˆˆ L(R), X = RX|S has multiple solutions
Because Îµ causes infinite loops in FA
Because concatenation is undefined with Îµ
explanation: If Îµ âˆˆ L(R), then X = RX|S may have multiple solutions. For example if R=Îµ, any X âŠ‡ S satisfies X = ÎµX|S = X|S. The uniqueness (X = R*S) is only guaranteed when Îµ âˆ‰ L(R), ensuring the equation has a unique least fixed point.

q: To apply Arden's theorem to a DFA with states {q0,q1,q2}, you write equations for:
explanation: To apply Arden's theorem (the state-equation method), you write one equation per state capturing the transitions into that state. Then you solve the system substituting equations to find the expression for the start state's equation, which gives the language of the FA.

q: For a DFA state qi, the equation is written as qi = (union of) Î±ijÂ·qj for each transition qj --Î±ij--> qi, plus Îµ if qi is an accepting state. What does Îµ represent here?
options: That qi accepts the empty string as input from itself
That the automaton can terminate at qi (qi is accepting)
explanation: In the state-equation method, Îµ is added to the equation of a state qi if qi is an accepting state. This represents the fact that when the automaton is in qi (having read some string x), if qi is accepting, x is in the language â€” the Îµ represents the option of stopping/accepting here.

q: Given the DFA: states {A,B}, start=A, accept={A}, Î´(A,0)=B, Î´(A,1)=A, Î´(B,0)=A, Î´(B,1)=B. What is the RE for this DFA using Arden's theorem?
explanation: Setting up equations: A = 0Â·B + 1Â·A + Îµ; B = 0Â·A + 1Â·B. Solve B: B = 1*0A (by Arden's, since Îµâˆ‰L(1)). Substitute into A: A = 0Â·1*0Â·A + 1Â·A + Îµ = (01*0|1)A + Îµ. By Arden's: A = (01*0|1)* = 1*(01*01*)* = 1*(01*0)*1*. This represents strings with even number of 0s.

q: The state-elimination method for FA â†’ RE works by:
options: Adding new states to the FA
Removing states one at a time and updating transition labels with REs
Converting the FA to a CFG first
explanation: The state-elimination method converts FA to RE by iteratively removing non-start, non-accepting states and updating the remaining transition labels with regular expressions that capture the eliminated paths. When only start and accepting states remain, the labels give the RE.

q: In the state-elimination method, when eliminating state q with self-loop R, incoming edge P, and outgoing edge Q, the bypass edge label becomes:
explanation: When state q has self-loop R, incoming edge P (from some state s to q), and outgoing edge Q (from q to some state t), after eliminating q the direct edge from s to t gets label PÂ·R*Â·Q. The self-loop R* accounts for any number of times we loop through q before leaving.

q: Which of the following is TRUE about the RE obtained from a DFA?
options: Multiple equivalent REs exist for the same language
The RE must use only union and concatenation
Kleene star cannot appear in the RE
explanation: A given regular language can be represented by infinitely many equivalent regular expressions. For example, a|a = a, a** = a*, etc. The RE obtained from a DFA is one valid representation, but not the only one. REs for non-trivial regular languages typically require Kleene star.

q: Arden's theorem can be applied to an NFA directly (without first converting to DFA).
options: True â€” the theorem works for any FA
False â€” it only works for DFA
True â€” but gives a non-deterministic RE
False â€” NFA states have ambiguous transitions
explanation: Arden's theorem (state-equation method) can be applied directly to NFAs as well as DFAs. The equations are set up the same way, treating multiple transitions on the same symbol as union in the equation. The resulting RE is deterministic regardless.

q: Which of the following operations is NOT closed under regular languages?
explanation: Regular languages are closed under union, intersection, complement, concatenation, and Kleene star. However, infinite union of regular languages is NOT guaranteed to be regular. For example, the infinite union {aâ¿bâ¿ | n=0}, {aâ¿bâ¿ | n=1}, {aâ¿bâ¿ | n=2}, ... gives {aâ¿bâ¿ | nâ‰¥0} which is not regular.

q: If L1 and L2 are regular languages, which of the following is always regular?
explanation: Regular languages are closed under both quotient (left/right) and set difference. Set difference L1\L2 = L1 âˆ© L2' (intersection with complement), and since regular languages are closed under intersection and complement, L1\L2 is regular. Left quotient is also a closure property.

q: The complement of a regular language is:
explanation: Regular languages are closed under complement. If L is regular and accepted by DFA M, then the complement L' is accepted by the DFA M' obtained by swapping accepting and non-accepting states. Therefore the complement of any regular language is always regular.

q: Which of the following closure properties does NOT hold for regular languages?
explanation: Regular languages are closed under reversal, homomorphism, and Kleene star. However, intersection of a regular language with a CFL gives a CFL (not necessarily regular). So 'intersection with a CFL' does not guarantee the result is regular â€” thus this is not a closure property of the class of regular languages itself.

q: If L is regular, then L^R (reversal of L) is:
explanation: Regular languages are closed under reversal. If L is accepted by DFA M, then L^R is accepted by an NFA obtained by reversing all transitions of M, swapping start and accepting states. Since every NFA has an equivalent DFA, L^R is regular.

q: The pumping lemma for regular languages states that for any regular language L, there exists a pumping length p such that any string s âˆˆ L with |s| â‰¥ p can be written as s = xyz where:
options: y â‰  Îµ, |xy| â‰¤ p, and xy^i z âˆˆ L for all i â‰¥ 0
x â‰  Îµ, |yz| â‰¤ p, and xy^i z âˆˆ L for all i â‰¥ 1
y â‰  Îµ, |y| â‰¤ p, and xy^i z âˆˆ L for all i â‰¥ 0
y = Îµ, |xy| â‰¤ p, and xyz âˆˆ L
explanation: The pumping lemma conditions are: (1) y â‰  Îµ (the pumped part is non-empty), (2) |xy| â‰¤ p (the pump occurs within first p characters), and (3) xy^iz âˆˆ L for all i â‰¥ 0 (pumping any number of times, including 0, stays in L).

q: To use the pumping lemma to prove L = {aâ¿bâ¿ | n â‰¥ 0} is not regular, the adversary (us) picks:
options: A specific string s âˆˆ L with |s| â‰¥ p
explanation: In using the pumping lemma to prove non-regularity: the pumping length p is given by the assumed regularity. We (the prover) choose a specific string s âˆˆ L with |s| â‰¥ p (typically s = aáµ–báµ–). The adversary then chooses the decomposition xyz. We must show that for EVERY such decomposition, some pumping violates membership in L.

q: For L = {aâ¿bâ¿ | n â‰¥ 0}, choosing s = aáµ–báµ–, the pumping lemma gives |xy| â‰¤ p, so xy consists entirely of:
explanation: Since |xy| â‰¤ p and s = aáµ–báµ– (first p characters are all a's), the substring xy lies entirely within the first p characters, which are all a's. Therefore y = aáµ for some k â‰¥ 1. Pumping y gives aáµ–âºáµbáµ– âˆ‰ L (unequal counts), contradicting L being regular.

q: Which of the following languages CAN be shown to be regular using the pumping lemma?
options: { w | w has equal a's and b's }
explanation: The pumping lemma can only DISPROVE regularity; it cannot prove a language IS regular (the pumping lemma is a necessary, not sufficient, condition for regularity). However, (ab)* IS regular (we know this from its RE directly). The question is a trap: among the options, only (ab)* is regular, but the pumping lemma alone cannot prove it.

q: If a language L satisfies the pumping lemma, what can we conclude?
options: L may or may not be regular
explanation: Satisfying the pumping lemma is a necessary but NOT sufficient condition for regularity. Some non-regular languages also satisfy the pumping lemma (e.g., {aâ¿báµcáµ | n,m â‰¥ 0} satisfies it). Therefore, satisfying the pumping lemma only tells us L may or may not be regular â€” we cannot conclude regularity from it alone.

q: A Context-Free Grammar (CFG) is formally defined as a 4-tuple. Which of the following correctly represents it?
options: (V, Î£, R, S) where V=variables, Î£=terminals, R=rules, S=start symbol
(Î£, V, S, R) where Î£=terminals, V=variables, S=start symbol, R=rules
(Q, Î£, Î´, q0) where Q=states, Î£=alphabet, Î´=transition, q0=start
(V, Î£, Î“, S) where Î“=stack alphabet
explanation: A CFG is formally a 4-tuple (V, Î£, R, S): V is the finite set of non-terminals (variables), Î£ is the finite set of terminals (disjoint from V), R is the finite set of production rules of the form Aâ†’Î± where AâˆˆV and Î±âˆˆ(VâˆªÎ£)*, and SâˆˆV is the start symbol. Option C describes a finite automaton, not a CFG.

q: In a Context-Free Grammar, every production rule must be of the form:
options: A â†’ Î±, where A is a non-terminal and Î± âˆˆ (V âˆª Î£)*
A â†’ B, where both A and B are non-terminals
a â†’ Î±, where a is a terminal and Î± âˆˆ (V âˆª Î£)*
A â†’ Î±, where A âˆˆ Î£ and Î± âˆˆ V*
explanation: In a CFG, each production rule has the form Aâ†’Î± where A is a single non-terminal (AâˆˆV) and Î± is any string over the combined alphabet of non-terminals and terminals, i.e., Î±âˆˆ(VâˆªÎ£)*. The left-hand side must always be a single non-terminal â€” this is what distinguishes context-free from context-sensitive grammars.

q: Which derivation strategy always expands the leftmost non-terminal in the sentential form?
explanation: In a leftmost derivation, at each step the leftmost non-terminal in the current sentential form is replaced using a production rule. This is denoted â‡’lm. Rightmost derivation (also called canonical derivation) always replaces the rightmost non-terminal. Bottom-up parsing reconstructs the derivation in reverse, but that does not define a 'bottom-up derivation' in the standard sense.

q: The rightmost derivation is also known as:
explanation: The rightmost derivation is also called the canonical derivation. In each step of a rightmost derivation, the rightmost non-terminal in the current sentential form is replaced. LR parsers (bottom-up) produce the rightmost derivation in reverse. This is a standard terminology in formal language theory and compiler design.

q: A parse tree for a CFG derivation has which property regarding its leaves?
options: The leaves, read left to right, form the derived string (yield of the tree)
The leaves represent only non-terminal symbols
The root of the tree is a terminal symbol
Each internal node is a terminal symbol
explanation: In a parse tree (also called a derivation tree), the root is the start symbol, internal nodes are non-terminals, and each node's children correspond to the right-hand side of the production applied. The leaves are terminal symbols (or Îµ), and reading them left-to-right gives the derived string â€” called the yield or frontier of the tree.

q: Which of the following statements about parse trees and derivations is TRUE?
options: A single parse tree may correspond to multiple leftmost derivations
One parse tree corresponds to exactly one leftmost derivation and one rightmost derivation
Every leftmost derivation produces a different parse tree
Parse trees are only defined for ambiguous grammars
explanation: A parse tree uniquely determines both a leftmost derivation and a rightmost derivation. Given a parse tree, you can read off exactly one leftmost derivation (always pick leftmost non-terminal) and exactly one rightmost derivation. Multiple derivations can yield the same parse tree only if you mix strategies, but the lm and rm derivations are each unique per tree.

q: A CFG is said to be ambiguous if:
options: Some string in the language has more than one parse tree
Every string has exactly one parse tree
The grammar generates an infinite language
explanation: A grammar G is ambiguous if there exists at least one string wâˆˆL(G) that has two or more distinct parse trees. Equivalently, w has more than one leftmost derivation (or more than one rightmost derivation). Ambiguity is a property of the grammar, not the language. Left recursion and infinite languages do not imply ambiguity.

q: Consider the grammar S â†’ S+S | S*S | a. The string a+a*a is:
options: Ambiguous in this grammar because it has two parse trees
Unambiguous because operator precedence is defined
Not in the language of the grammar
Generated by exactly one leftmost derivation
explanation: The grammar Sâ†’S+S|S*S|a is the classic example of an ambiguous grammar. The string a+a*a can be parsed as (a+a)*a or a+(a*a), giving two distinct parse trees. Since no precedence is encoded in the grammar, both derivations are valid. This is why expression grammars must encode precedence through grammar levels.

q: An inherently ambiguous language is one where:
options: Every CFG for that language is ambiguous
There exists at least one unambiguous CFG for it
explanation: A context-free language is inherently ambiguous if every possible CFG that generates it is ambiguous â€” there is no unambiguous grammar for that language. An example is {a^i b^j c^k | i=j or j=k}. This is different from an ambiguous grammar, which may have an equivalent unambiguous grammar for the same language.

q: Which of the following grammars is UNAMBIGUOUS for arithmetic expressions respecting standard precedence?
explanation: The grammar Eâ†’E+T|T, Tâ†’T*F|F, Fâ†’(E)|id is the standard unambiguous grammar for arithmetic expressions. It encodes precedence (multiplication binds tighter than addition) and left-associativity through the grammar structure. Eâ†’E+E|E*E|id is the classic ambiguous grammar that does not encode precedence or associativity.

q: To design a CFG for the language L = {a^n b^n | n â‰¥ 1}, which production set is correct?
explanation: The language {a^n b^n | nâ‰¥1} is the canonical context-free (but not regular) language. The correct CFG is Sâ†’aSb|ab. For n=1: Sâ‡’ab. For n=2: Sâ‡’aSbâ‡’aabb. The rule Sâ†’aSb wraps one more 'a' and 'b' around a shorter balanced string. Option D is not a valid production rule syntax.

q: A CFG for the language of all palindromes over {a,b} (including empty string) is:
options: S â†’ aSa | bSb | a | b | Îµ
S â†’ aSa | bSb | ab | ba
explanation: Palindromes over {a,b} include Îµ, single characters (a,b), and strings where the first and last characters match and the middle is also a palindrome. The CFG Sâ†’aSa|bSb|a|b|Îµ captures all cases: Îµ for the empty palindrome, a and b for single-character palindromes, aSa and bSb for longer ones. Option D generates strings where first and last characters differ, which are not palindromes.

q: A useless symbol in a CFG is one that:
options: Either cannot be derived from the start symbol, or cannot derive any terminal string
Appears only on the right-hand side of productions
Is a terminal symbol not used in any production
Appears more than once in any single production
explanation: A symbol X is useless if it either (1) is non-generating: cannot derive any string of terminals (no terminal string is reachable from X), or (2) is non-reachable: cannot be reached from the start symbol S in any derivation. Both conditions make X useless. The standard simplification removes non-generating symbols first, then non-reachable symbols, in that order.

q: When eliminating useless symbols from a CFG, the correct order of steps is:
options: First remove non-generating symbols, then remove non-reachable symbols
First remove non-reachable symbols, then remove non-generating symbols
Order does not matter; the result is the same
explanation: The correct order is: (1) remove non-generating symbols (those that cannot derive any terminal string), then (2) remove non-reachable symbols (those that cannot be reached from the start symbol). If you reverse the order, you may incorrectly keep symbols that become unreachable after removing non-generating ones, leading to an incorrect simplification. Order matters!

q: Which of the following is a non-generating symbol in the grammar: Sâ†’AB|a, Aâ†’aA|a, Bâ†’bB?
explanation: A symbol is non-generating if it cannot derive any string consisting only of terminals. Bâ†’bB: the only production for B introduces B again â€” there is no base case.
